scenario: An RPA bot interacting with a legacy app fails sporadically. Team wants automatic screenshot capture on failure to debug.
question: RPA screenshot-on-failure for debugging?

options.text: md:26); screenshot-on-failure is a Robot Framework feature. SeleniumLibrary auto-captures a page screenshot on any failed keyword by default (screenshot_on_failure = True); BrowserLibrary and AppiumLibrary have similar built-ins. md:63-65) -- upload screenshots to external storage (S3 + URL variable) before the job finishes to preserve them. md:151-167); an Error Boundary on the RPA Task can route to a manual-review User Task that displays the screenshot URL in a Form field. Privacy: screenshots may capture PII -- restrict access, gate by worker label, or skip on sensitive screens.

RPA screenshot capture on failure requires significant additional configuration involving custom Robot Framework listeners, third-party screenshot libraries, and manual integration code to store screenshots in a location accessible for debugging. While the exact configuration details depend on the Robot Framework library version and the specific RPA runtime, the SeleniumLibrary and other common Robot Framework UI libraries include built-in screenshot-on-failure capabilities that are enabled by default or with minimal configuration, not requiring custom listener development.

RPA screenshot-on-failure always captures the full screen, including all open applications and windows, with no option to limit the capture to the target application window or a specific UI region. Many Robot Framework UI libraries support configurable screenshot scope. Depending on the library and configuration, screenshot capture can be scoped to the specific browser window, application window, or region being automated, rather than always requiring a full-screen capture that may include unrelated content or sensitive information.

Robot Framework scripts can only capture screenshots at explicitly coded points using manual Take Screenshot keyword calls; there is no automatic on-failure capture, meaning any unexpected failure that was not anticipated in the script leaves no visual evidence for debugging. This overstates the limitation. Robot Framework's SeleniumLibrary and similar UI automation libraries automatically capture a screenshot whenever a keyword fails, as part of the built-in failure reporting mechanism. This automatic capture happens without any explicit Take Screenshot keyword in the script.

a.text: Correct. RPA screenshot-on-failure patterns: Auto-capture mechanisms (typical): Exception hooks: framework catches uncaught exceptions; captures screenshot before propagating. Per-step capture: optional screenshots after each action for full visual trace. On-demand capture: bot code calls screenshot() explicitly at points of interest. Configuration: Enabled / disabled: typically configurable per project. Capture scope: full screen vs target window vs region. Resolution: native vs reduced (for storage efficiency). Format: PNG / JPEG. Storage: Local: bot worker writes to filesystem; later uploaded. Cloud: directly to S3 / Azure Blob. Camunda integration: screenshot URL / ID stored in process variables for BPMN visibility. Retention: Driven by privacy / cost / debugging needs. Critical failures: keep long-term. Routine successes: short-term or none. Privacy + security considerations: PII risk: screenshots may show customer data, passwords, internal screens. Redaction: difficult automatically; better to skip screenshots when on sensitive screens. Access control: restrict who can view screenshots (audit log access). Compliance: GDPR / HIPAA may require explicit handling rules. Integration with BPMN: Bot completion: returns variables including screenshot reference. Error Boundary on RPA Task: catches bot failure; downstream process can: Route to manual review with screenshot displayed in form. Send alert to ops with screenshot attached. Trigger retry with diagnostic info. Debugging workflow: 1. RPA bot fails. 2. Screenshot captured + uploaded. 3. Process Incident in Operate with screenshot URL. 4. Engineer opens Operate; clicks link; sees screenshot. 5. Diagnoses (e.g., "popup blocked the action" or "page changed structure"). 6. Fixes bot or retries. Verify exact RPA features per Camunda 8 RPA edition / version.
